chapters.title: You can put obesity behind you for good
chapters.text:
Few topics in nutrition and medicine get more attention than obesity. A large share of the population lives with it, and millions of books, articles, and courses online claim to melt away extra fat.

Many of those guides have no real science behind them. They come from trial and error and lean on testimonials to sell. How Not to Diet stands apart, since the summary is built on decades of solid research.

Michael Greger is a researcher and teacher who digs up the facts and lays them out for everyone. His book grew out of a long research project with his team. This summary condenses the most useful takeaways, so settle in and enjoy the ride.

When it comes to weight loss, science based methods are the only real bet you can make.

Start with the basics. What is obesity, and how do you know if you have it. Most people think they know, but they do not.

Extra weight alone does not make someone obese. There is a difference between being overweight and being obese. Obesity starts at a body mass index of 30 or more. A BMI between 25 and 29.9 is overweight. A BMI between 18.5 and 24.9 is in the healthy range.

You can check your own BMI with an online tool or do the math by hand. Multiply your weight in pounds by 703, then divide the result twice by your height in inches.

For example, a 250 pound person who is 70 inches tall has a BMI of 35.9.

Obesity starts at a BMI of 30. A BMI between 25 and 29.9 is overweight.

chapters.title: The food industry drives the rise in obesity
chapters.text:
Plenty of people feel confused. Have humans always been heavy, or did this start recently.

The honest answer needs a quick look back. Historical records show that obesity has always existed. The big shift came in the 1970s, when obesity in America and other rich countries jumped from about 1 in 30 people to about 1 in 3.

Many theories try to explain the rise, but none hold up well. The real driver is the food industry. The way we eat has changed dramatically since the 1970s, fueled by new machines that made fast food possible. Food makers also got smarter about producing sweet, high calorie products. We are wired to love sweet and rich food, so it became easy for almost anyone to gain weight. It was only a matter of time.

The low nutrition diet most of us eat today is the leading cause of obesity.

Beyond appearance and comfort, obesity carries serious health risks. The most common include arthritis, lower back pain, blood pressure problems, diabetes, fertility issues, and heart disease. The good news is that you can reverse obesity and the troubles that come with it through proven lifestyle changes, covered in the next chapters.

People who lie to themselves about investing are the same as overweight people who blame their genes for their weight, as Robert Kiyosaki has said.

Most of the world now lives in countries where being overweight or obese kills more people than being underweight.

chapters.title: Whole natural foods are the most reliable answer to obesity
chapters.text:
There is a reason people who lived thousands of years ago were healthier than we are. They ate better. That is the whole answer.

We may be more advanced, but we have lost touch with the simple ingredients our bodies need. The major building blocks of a weight loss diet are listed below.

Fiber

For a long time, fiber was ignored as a weight loss tool. The focus was on protein, with little attention to the one ingredient that should always come with it. Studies show that under 3 percent of Americans eat enough fiber, while more than 97 percent eat enough protein.

Fiber has many benefits, but two stand out. First, fiber fills the stomach, so you eat fewer calories than you burn. Second, fiber feeds the helpful bacteria in the gut. Those bacteria help with vitamin production, and in return the body feeds and shelters them.

Fiber is one of the most important weight loss ingredients, and most diets leave it out.

Fiber is essential for weight loss, and not many people know it. The situation is clear when the top fiber source in America turns out to be french fries. Some even think fiber comes from steak and other animal foods. The truth is that plants are the only real source of fiber. Fruits, legumes, and whole grains are the best options.

Water rich foods

Michael Greger points out that water rich foods add bulk without adding calories. Vegetables and fruits are packed with water, fill you up quickly, and curb the urge to reach for high calorie snacks.

Most vegetables are over 90 percent water, and fruits average around 80 percent. A good diet leans heavily on both. Processed foods like candy, pizza, and burgers have very little water, so you can keep eating them without feeling full.

Meat and bread sit around 30 percent water. Drinking a big glass of water with them helps for a moment, but it does not make the meal more filling in a meaningful way.

Look for foods that are low in fat and added sugar. Leafy greens like spinach, lettuce, and kale, plus beans, legumes, and fruits all fit.

Water rich foods like fruits and vegetables are powerful tools against obesity.

Extra salt and refined grains also work against you. Both are calorie dense and do not help weight loss.

Doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results is insanity, a line usually linked to Albert Einstein.

The top seven water rich foods are cucumber, tomatoes, spinach, broccoli, brussels sprouts, oranges, and apples.

chapters.title: Your own cravings can work against you
chapters.text:
A study from Columbia University found that without pleasure, people eat only the calories their body actually needs. Researchers used two groups, one with obesity and one at a normal weight. Both could eat from a feeding machine whenever they wanted, as much or as little as they liked.

Normal weight participants ate the right number of calories without trying. The surprising finding was that participants with obesity ate less than the normal weight group. This flew in the face of the idea that obesity is always about overeating. The takeaway was that, without pleasure cues, the body can limit how much someone who is overweight actually consumes.

Does that mean people with obesity are fighting their own bodies on purpose. In part, yes. It is a little more layered than that. The body has two appetite systems. The homeostatic system keeps weight steady, while the hedonic system chases pleasure and taste. When food lights up sight, smell, and taste, the hedonic system pushes you to eat more than your stomach can hold.

The same trigger fires when you see a wide variety of appealing foods.

A varied selection of healthy foods keeps the drive to eat well alive.

The food industry knows this well, and uses it to sell more junk. The drive is built into us, so fighting it head on is hard. The smarter move is to use it for your own goals. Keep a colorful spread of fruits and vegetables at home, and learn to cook them in ways that look and taste great.

There is no love sincerer than the love of food, as George Bernard Shaw once put it.

chapters.title: Accountability is a powerful weight loss tool
chapters.text:
By now it is clear that plant based diets work better than diets full of animal products. Some people find that healthy plants alone do not move the needle fast enough. The reason is that it is easy to slip when no one is watching.

Most habits do not change easily, since they have built up over years. If you want better feedback on your weight loss, hold yourself accountable. Telling the people around you is a good first step. You can also keep a daily log of your progress.

Accountability is what keeps you on track, even when relapsing feels tempting.

You can build accountability by stepping on a scale often. Experts suggest once or twice a day. You can also join a support group or pick a friend or coach to report to. The stronger the accountability around you, the better your chances of keeping the weight off. Along with a scale and a support system, other tools for faster weight loss include appetite suppressants like saffron, black pepper, and cumin.

At the end of the day we answer to ourselves, and our success comes from what we do, in the words of Catherine Pulsifer.

chapters.title: Chronobiology can support steady weight loss
chapters.text:
Chronobiology is a branch of biology that studies how the rhythms of the sun, moon, and seasons shape our physical, mental, and emotional cycles.

How solid are these claims. There is enough scientific evidence to show that seasonal and environmental shifts do affect the body and mind. Still, the topic carries plenty of myths. For example, a common belief says that skipping breakfast helps with weight loss. The reasoning is that skipping a meal means fewer calories to deal with during the day, but that is just wrong. If you plan to skip any meal on a regular basis, dinner is the better choice, since late night food often does not digest well before morning.

Skipping breakfast does not cause weight loss, but skipping dinner might help.

A few chronobiology habits support weight loss. Sleep at night so you can be active during the day. Get 6 to 7 hours of sleep. Go to bed early. Do not go to bed right after dinner. Aim to finish dinner at least two and a half hours before bed.

chapters.title: Dr. Michael Greger's guide to eating well
chapters.text:
It is fitting to close this summary with Dr. Greger's expert advice on how to eat. The guideline is the product of decades of research on how food and eating habits shape the results your body produces.

First, Dr. Greger suggests drinking two cups of unflavored water before a meal. Follow that with what he calls negative calorie foods, items like fruits and vegetables that fill you up without adding many calories.

You can use vinegar if you like, but not straight. Add it to a dressing or mix it into a drink. Finally, eat without distractions. Turn off the TV, set the phone aside, and just eat. Take your time. Studies show that the longer people spend at a meal, the less they tend to overeat. So cut distractions and slow down. Chew more, take smaller bites, whatever works for you. Just do not rush. Dr. Greger calls this the 21 minute rule. Try not to finish a meal in under 21 minutes.

Dr. Greger also suggests the following day and night habits. Take a quarter teaspoon of black cumin once a day. Add a quarter teaspoon of garlic powder once a day. Use a teaspoon of ground ginger once a day. Take two teaspoons of nutritional yeast once a day. Use two teaspoons of cumin with lunch and dinner. Drink three cups of green tea a day.

For the evening, start your fast by 7 pm. Aim for 6 to 7 hours of sleep.

It took more than a day to put weight on, and it will take more than a day to take it off, as the saying goes.

chapters.title: A practical path to lasting weight loss
chapters.text:
Obesity affects millions of people around the world. There is no single, clean solution, which is why people with obesity jump from one shiny plan to the next with little to show for it. Many have lost hope that their weight can ever be fixed.

Steady and healthy weight loss is possible with science based strategies. You do not have to grind through gym sessions if that is not your style. This summary has walked through several tools you can use to reach a healthy weight. They work whether your weight gain came from a medical condition, life events, or family history.

The first habit to embrace is a plant based diet. Studies keep showing that plant based plans work better than animal based ones. The reason many people find it hard to stick with plants is that the food feels plain. You can fix that by making the meals more interesting on your own. Add more fruits and vegetables to every meal, especially water rich fruits like watermelon, apples, and oranges. Sleep well at night so you do not crash in the afternoon, and finish eating at least two and a half hours before bed.

Try this

Follow the 21 minute rule. Spend at least 21 minutes on each meal from now on. There is no point rushing.
